about: Starter outlines for practice only—not official AP exam content. For course frameworks and skills, use the College Board at https://apstudents.collegeboard.org/courses

topics.title: APUSH — Period 1 (1491–1607)
topics.content:
Overview: Before sustained European settlement, North America hosted diverse societies whose economies, religions, and governments reflected local environments and long-distance networks.

Key ideas: maize-based agriculture in many regions; trade routes linking peoples; kinship, confederacy, and city-based political forms; early European exploration reconfigures Atlantic geography without yet dominating continents.

Vocabulary & anchors: long-distance exchange; adaptation; Columbian Exchange (preview—full weight in later periods); primary sources from multiple regions, not only English colonies.

Timeline thread: 1491 baseline → first contact zones → Spanish/French/English probes with different goals (trade vs. settlement vs. extraction).

Study focus: compare two regions (e.g., Southwest vs. Northeast) on food, labor, and leadership. Name one non-European trade connection that predates Columbus.

Common mistakes: treating Indigenous North America as a single culture; skipping environmental and gendered labor patterns.

Exam tip: practice HIPP-style sourcing and tie claims to historical thinking—causation, continuity/change, comparison.

topics.title: APUSH — Period 2 (1607–1754)
topics.content:
Overview: European colonies take root; African slavery becomes central to labor in some regions; Native diplomacy and resistance persist; consumer goods and ideas circulate in an emerging Atlantic world.

Key ideas: Chesapeake vs. New England vs. middle colonies; indentured servitude vs. chattel slavery; mercantilism; Great Awakening; wars for empire (Seven Years’ War as hinge).

Vocabulary & anchors: Bacon’s Rebellion context; Navigation Acts; Iroquois Confederacy diplomacy; Stono Rebellion (preview of resistance patterns).

Study focus: explain how labor systems and land policy shaped distinct colonial societies.

Common mistakes: flattening “colonists” into one ideology; ignoring Native power and legal treaties.

Exam tip: compare two colonies using two different categories (economy + religion, or labor + politics).

topics.title: APUSH — Period 3 (1754–1800)
topics.content:
Overview: Imperial wars and British reforms after 1763 spark colonial protest; independence produces experiments in republican government and debates over who counts as “the people.”

Key ideas: French and Indian War legacies; taxes, boycotts, and committees of correspondence; military strategy and foreign aid; Articles weaknesses; Constitutional compromises; Federalist vs. Anti-Federalist arguments.

Vocabulary & anchors: virtual representation; loyalism; Valley Forge; checks and balances; three-fifths compromise (interpret debates, not moralize in one sentence).

Study focus: trace sovereignty—from Parliament’s claims to “the people” in state constitutions to the federal design.

Common mistakes: reading the Constitution only as a rights document without powers/federalism.

Exam tip: thesis + two concrete evidence chunks from different colonies or groups (e.g., artisans vs. planters).

topics.title: APUSH — Period 4 (1800–1848)
topics.content:
Overview: The new republic expands territorially and commercially; market revolution transforms work; reformers target slavery, prisons, education, and women’s roles—often in tension with each other.

Key ideas: Louisiana Purchase; transportation revolution; cotton and enslaved labor; Second Great Awakening; abolitionism; early women’s rights (Seneca Falls as capstone example).

Vocabulary & anchors: Missouri Compromise; Monroe Doctrine (motives vs. myth); nativism seeds; Lowell mills as gendered labor.

Study focus: connect market growth to sectional stress—how does western expansion embed slavery’s politics?

Common mistakes: “North good / South bad” caricature before 1850; ignoring free Black communities and organizing.

Exam tip: one reform movement as continuity/change case study with limits of reform.

topics.title: APUSH — Period 7 (1890–1945)
topics.content:
Overview: Industrial scale, empire, migration, and two world wars recast the federal government’s role and Americans’ everyday lives—from factory floors to trenches to breadlines to assembly lines again.

Key ideas: Populism and Progressivism targets; Jim Crow and Great Migration; WWI mobilization and Red Summer context; New Deal alphabet agencies; WWII home front and internment; beginnings of modern foreign policy institutions.

Vocabulary & anchors: Sherman Antitrust Act usage; Square Deal vs. New Deal; lend-lease; Double V campaign.

Study focus: compare Progressive reform goals with measurable outcomes; link foreign crises to domestic dissent or consensus.

Common mistakes: listing New Deal programs without goals/constraints; treating WWII as only European theater.

Exam tip: LEQ on federal power—pick two eras and evaluate change vs. continuity with evidence.

topics.title: APUSH — Period 8 (1945–1980)
topics.content:
Overview: Cold War, civil rights struggles, suburbanization, and rights expansions redefine citizenship; economic growth and stagflation reshape expectations of government and work.

Key ideas: containment; Korean and Vietnam wars; Brown and civil rights coalitions; Great Society; second-wave feminism; environmentalism; détente and oil shocks.

Vocabulary & anchors: GI Bill; Fair Deal vs. New Frontier vs. Great Society; EPA founding context.

Study focus: connect foreign policy commitments to domestic protest or compliance.

Common mistakes: treating “the Sixties” as only youth culture; skipping conservative and religious mobilizations.

Exam tip: DBQ practice—sort docs by author, audience, purpose before thesis.

topics.title: AP World — Renaissance & global encounters
topics.content:
Overview: European states and elites fund art, science, and navigation while gunpowder empires and Asian trade hubs remain powerful centers—global contact intensifies but is not yet European-dominated everywhere.

Key ideas: humanism and patronage; printing press diffusion; Iberian Atlantic missions and conquest; African kingdoms and trade; Ming/Qing policies toward maritime trade.

Vocabulary & anchors: joint-stock companies (early); mestizo societies; syncretism in religion.

Study focus: pair one European cultural shift with one non-European state-building or trade policy from the same century.

Common mistakes: Renaissance as apolitical “rebirth”; ignoring African and Indigenous agency in Atlantic systems.

Exam tip: comparison thesis with one similarity and one difference anchored in evidence.

topics.title: AP World — Global trade (1450–1750)
topics.content:
Overview: Silver from the Americas funds Asian economies; Indian Ocean and trans-Saharan networks persist; Atlantic slave trade expands coerced migration that reshapes labor in the Americas.

Key ideas: Manila galleons; mercantilist competition; chartered monopolies; plantation complexes; demographic catastrophe and resistance in the Americas.

Vocabulary & anchors: middle passage; asiento system (context); encomienda vs. hacienda (compare carefully).

Study focus: write a causation paragraph: American silver → spending in China/Europe → one political effect.

Common mistakes: treating “globalization” as only technology; skipping environmental costs of mining and sugar.

Exam tip: map one commodity chain across three regions with dates.

topics.title: AP World — Industrialization & imperialism
topics.content:
Overview: Coal, steam, and later steel reorder production, time, and urban life; empires justify extraction with racial ideology while colonized peoples adapt, resist, and reform.

Key ideas: second industrial revolution markers; labor unions and reform laws; technologies of rule (railways, telegraphs); Indian National Congress–era claims; Ethiopian resistance as counterexample.

Vocabulary & anchors: Berlin Conference (Africa scramble context); concessions in China; Young Turks as reform nationalism example.

Study focus: link factory demand to one colonial commodity and one local response.

Common mistakes: imperialism as only “Europe vs. world”; ignoring Japanese Meiji industrial state.

Exam tip: causation chain—resource + technology + political outcome—with dates.

topics.title: AP World — 1914–present (war, peace, globalization)
topics.content:
Overview: Total war, self-determination rhetoric, decolonization, Cold War bipolarity, and post-1989 institutions create today’s dense migration, finance, and rights language—often contested.

Key ideas: League vs. UN ambitions; decolonization timelines; non-aligned movement; Bretton Woods basics; human rights covenants vs. sovereignty claims; climate and health as global commons problems.

Vocabulary & anchors: apartheid; proxy wars; neoliberalism (define carefully in your own words); ASEAN-style regionalism (example).

Study focus: compare post-1945 economic integration in two world regions.

Common mistakes: Cold War reduced to nukes only; skipping economic Cold War (oil, debt).

Exam tip: group DBQ sources by perspective before thesis; practice one counterargument paragraph.

topics.title: AP Euro — Renaissance & reformations
topics.content:
Overview: City-states and print culture spread new humanist curricula; religious reform splits Latin Christendom into competing confessions that reshape politics and daily life.

Key ideas: Italian vs. Northern Renaissance emphases; Lutheran, Calvinist, and Catholic Reform paths; wars of religion as state-building catalysts.

Vocabulary & anchors: indulgence debate; Peace of Augsburg; politiques (conceptual label).

Study focus: compare one artistic patronage goal with one reformer’s educational goal.

Common mistakes: treating Protestantism as monolithic; ignoring Catholic reform energy.

Exam tip: LEQ—change in political authority tied to religious identity with two states.

topics.title: AP Euro — Scientific Revolution & Enlightenment
topics.content:
Overview: New methods (mathematics, observation, institutions) challenge Aristotelian cosmos; salons and encyclopedias spread critical ideas that collide with absolutist courts.

Key ideas: heliocentrism controversy; Bacon/Descartes methods; separation of powers and social contract language; enlightened absolutism as hybrid.

Vocabulary & anchors: Royal academies; philosophes; censorship vs. underground circulation.

Study focus: connect one scientific claim to one political pamphlet argument of the 1700s.

Common mistakes: “science replaced religion” oversimplification; skipping women’s roles in salons and science.

Exam tip: practice attributing ideas to named thinkers without anachronism.

topics.title: AP Euro — Industrial society & mass politics
topics.content:
Overview: Factories, unions, suffrage expansions, and nationalism turn subjects into citizens—while imperial competition and World War I break old diplomatic assumptions.

Key ideas: British reform acts; socialist and anarchist currents; Realpolitik; alliance system failures; wartime total mobilization.

Vocabulary & anchors: Zollverein (German customs context); Chartism; February/October revolutions (sketch causes).

Study focus: explain how one reform narrowed or widened the electorate and who resisted.

Common mistakes: industrialization as only Britain; ignoring peasant politics east of Elbe.

Exam tip: causation paragraph from economic change to one party outcome.

topics.title: AP Gov — Constitutional principles
topics.content:
Overview: The Constitution emerges from compromise over power—who can tax, regulate commerce, enforce laws, and amend the frame—while Federalist essays defend a workable but still contested design.

Key ideas: enumerated vs. implied vs. reserved powers; checks in appointments, vetoes, and judicial review practice; federalism as contested balance, not a fixed ratio.

Vocabulary & anchors: elastic clause; supremacy clause; anti-federalist fears; Marbury as precedent story (rule of law vs. politics).

Study focus: apply a school-policy scenario: which two branches check each other and through which article/section logic?

Common mistakes: Articles of Confederation weaknesses mixed up with later federalism fights.

Exam tip: define devolution, mandate, and commerce power crisply; use at least one court case name correctly.

topics.title: AP Gov — Civil liberties & civil rights
topics.content:
Overview: Rights talk expands from Bill of Rights limits on national power to incorporation against states and to equality claims tested in courts, Congress, and movements.

Key ideas: selective incorporation timeline; speech time/place/manner; establishment tests (overview level); equal protection tiers; disparate impact vs. discriminatory intent.

Vocabulary & anchors: Gitlow gateway; strict scrutiny vs. rational basis; Civil Rights Act titles (big-picture).

Study focus: compare two amendments used in similar fact patterns (e.g., speech vs. assembly).

Common mistakes: civil liberties vs. civil rights used interchangeably.

Exam tip: one case—state majority’s core worry vs. dissent’s worry in plain English.

topics.title: AP Gov — Elections, parties, & media
topics.content:
Overview: Linkage institutions translate public opinion into nominees and policy agendas—unevenly—while money, rules, and media frames tilt who is seen as “electable.”

Key ideas: primary types; delegate math basics; FECA/BCRA themes; Super PAC independence (legal vs. practical); polarization measures; agenda setting vs. framing.

Vocabulary & anchors: coattails; safe seats; motor voter; selective exposure online.

Study focus: how would a shift from caucuses to primaries change candidate incentives?

Common mistakes: “both parties identical”; ignoring state law variation.

Exam tip: FRQ—name a mechanism (e.g., electoral college) not vague “people vote.”

topics.title: AP Gov — Bureaucracy & public policy
topics.content:
Overview: Congress writes vague statutes; agencies fill details through rulemaking; courts and Congress push back—policy outcomes depend on discretion, oversight, and iron triangles or issue networks.

Key ideas: rulemaking notice-and-comment; discretionary authority; oversight tools (hearings, GAO, appropriations); policy types (distributive/redistributive/regulatory).

Vocabulary & anchors: iron triangle vs. issue network; discretionary vs. mandatory spending (intro).

Study focus: trace one clean air or education policy from statute → agency rule → compliance challenge.

Common mistakes: bureaucracy as only “waste”—ignore implementation realities.

Exam tip: use a policy cycle label (agenda → formulation → implementation) in one sentence.

topics.title: AP Bio — Chemistry of life & cells
topics.content:
Overview: Life’s chemistry depends on water’s hydrogen bonding, carbon’s versatility, and polymers built from monomers; cells compartmentalize reactions and exchange matter with surroundings.

Key ideas: cohesion/adhesion/high specific heat; dehydration synthesis vs. hydrolysis; protein folding and enzyme active sites; competitive vs. noncompetitive inhibition; fluid mosaic membrane; passive vs. active transport; endocytosis/exocytosis.

Vocabulary & anchors: denaturation; saturation kinetics intuition; aquaporins; Na/K pump as primary active transport example.

Study focus: explain surface area-to-volume ratio as a limit on cell size with one calculation idea (qualitative).

Common mistakes: hypertonic/hypotonic direction of water; confusing channel and carrier proteins.

Exam tip: label cytosol vs. extracellular on osmosis sketches for plant and animal cells.

topics.title: AP Bio — Cell energetics
topics.content:
Overview: Photosynthesis traps light energy in chemical bonds; cellular respiration releases that energy in controlled steps to make ATP—organisms often link the two through shared molecules (O₂, CO₂, glucose).

Key ideas: redox in NAD⁺/NADH and FAD/FADH₂; glycolysis location and net; pyruvate oxidation; Krebs outputs; ETC chemiosmosis; light reactions vs. Calvin cycle; photorespiration as tradeoff mention.

Vocabulary & anchors: oxidative phosphorylation; cyclic vs. linear electron flow (big-picture); anaerobic fermentation products.

Study focus: track carbon skeleton count at high level (glucose → pyruvate → acetyl-CoA).

Common mistakes: plants don’t respire; “energy stored in ATP bonds” sloppy phrasing—prefer phosphorylation potential.

Exam tip: compare lactate fermentation vs. ethanol fermentation contexts (muscle vs. yeast).

topics.title: AP Bio — Heredity & gene expression
topics.content:
Overview: Mendelian rules describe idealized inheritance; linkage and crossing over complicate them; DNA replication preserves sequence; expression converts DNA to RNA to protein with multiple regulation points.

Key ideas: probability rules; incomplete dominance vs. codominance; testcross logic; semiconservative replication; leading/lagging strands; transcription factors; operon logic; mutations vs. chromosomal changes (intro).

Vocabulary & anchors: heterozygote advantage; Chi-square “fail to reject null”; wobble (name only).

Study focus: Punnett + probability for two genes (with and without linkage language).

Common mistakes: DNA pol vs. RNA pol roles; mixing translation elongation factors with replication clamps.

Exam tip: write hypotheses and df for Chi-square in one line before calculating.

topics.title: AP Bio — Natural selection & evolution
topics.content:
Overview: Populations change when heritable variation interacts with environment; Hardy–Weinberg models a null of no evolution; speciation mechanisms connect genetics to lineage splitting.

Key ideas: variation, selection, inheritance, time; directional/disruptive/stabilizing selection; gene flow vs. genetic drift; allopatric vs. sympatric speciation sketches.

Vocabulary & anchors: fitness; bottleneck; founder effect; reproductive isolation types (pre/post zygotic).

Study focus: explain antibiotic resistance as evolution in days–years scale.

Common mistakes: “individual evolved”; Lamarck language sneaking in.

Exam tip: when using H–W, state assumptions explicitly (no selection, random mating, etc.).

topics.title: AP Micro — Supply, demand, & elasticity
topics.content:
Overview: Prices coordinate decentralized choices; elasticities summarize responsiveness and predict tax incidence and revenue effects.

Key ideas: law of demand vs. inferior/Giffen exceptions (name only); shifts vs. movements; consumer/producer surplus; price floors/ceilings and deadweight loss; elasticity along linear demand intuition.

Vocabulary & anchors: normal vs. inferior good; total revenue test; binding vs. non-binding price control.

Study focus: draw binding ceiling with shortage and DWL narrative.

Common mistakes: shifting both curves without story; mixing up elasticity of demand vs. supply.

Exam tip: always label axes (P vertical, Q horizontal) and mark old vs. new equilibrium.

topics.title: AP Micro — Market structures
topics.content:
Overview: Number of firms, product differentiation, and entry barriers shape pricing power and long-run profit possibilities.

Key ideas: MR = MC profit max; P vs. MR in monopoly; zero economic profit in monopolistic competition long run; game theory payoff intuition in duopoly.

Vocabulary & anchors: price discrimination conditions; Nash equilibrium (definition); contestable markets (intro).

Study focus: table comparing perfect competition, mono comp, oligopoly, monopoly on firms, product, entry, long-run profit.

Common mistakes: branding alone = monopoly; forgetting average total cost in profit rectangles.

Exam tip: show shutdown point (P < AVC) on firm graph when relevant.

topics.title: AP Micro — Externalities & public goods
topics.content:
Overview: Private marginal cost can diverge from social marginal cost; policy tries to internalize externalities or fund non-excludable goods.

Key ideas: MSC/MPC graph; Pigouvian tax/subsidy intuition; Coase theorem conditions (low transaction costs, clear rights); public goods free-rider problem.

Vocabulary & anchors: tragedy of the commons (resource vs. public good distinction).

Study focus: one graph with efficient vs. market quantity for negative externality.

Common mistakes: calling any dislike an externality—need third-party effect.

Exam tip: label deadweight loss triangle between MSC and MB at market Q.

topics.title: AP Macro — GDP, inflation, & unemployment
topics.content:
Overview: National income accounting aggregates production and income; price level changes complicate comparisons; unemployment types help diagnose the business cycle.

Key ideas: expenditure approach components; real vs. nominal GDP; CPI vs. GDP deflator (uses); frictional/structural/cyclical unemployment; Okun’s law intuition.

Vocabulary & anchors: underground economy limits; seasonal adjustment (name only); labor force participation.

Study focus: explain why rising GDP can coexist with rising unemployment briefly (composition/timing).

Common mistakes: treating CPI as cost of living perfectly; confusing unemployment rate numerator/denominator.

Exam tip: define every ratio before plugging numbers on FRQs.

topics.title: AP Macro — AD–AS & fiscal policy
topics.content:
Overview: Aggregate demand and short/long-run aggregate supply frame recessions and inflation; fiscal stabilizers and discretionary policy shift AD with lags and debt tradeoffs.

Key ideas: wealth, interest-rate, and exchange-rate effects on AD; SRAS shocks (supply shocks); stagflation story; multiplier intuition; crowding out.

Vocabulary & anchors: automatic stabilizers; contractionary vs. expansionary policy; built-in stabilizers.

Study focus: draw recessionary gap and one policy closure with LRAS anchor.

Common mistakes: moving LRAS for every demand shock; forgetting price level axis.

Exam tip: narrate step-by-step what happens to r, I, and NX if not holding everything constant—pick one channel.

topics.title: AP Macro — Money, banking, & monetary policy
topics.content:
Overview: Money functions and fractional reserve banking link to money multiplier intuition; central bank tools aim at interest rates and credit conditions to steer AD.

Key ideas: M1/M2 ideas; balance sheet basics for banks; open market operations; discount rate; reserve requirements; Taylor rule (high level); quantitative easing as nontraditional tool name.

Vocabulary & anchors: liquidity trap (concept); federal funds rate as target.

Study focus: show how bond purchase increases reserves and lowers interest rates in a simple chain.

Common mistakes: saying “print money” for every expansion; mixing real and nominal rates.

Exam tip: distinguish loanable funds vs. liquidity preference stories when a prompt specifies the market.

topics.title: APHG — Thinking geographically
topics.content:
Overview: Geographers combine spatial data, scales, and human decision-making to explain patterns like clustering, diffusion barriers, and perceived regions.

Key ideas: scale of analysis; map projections and distortion tradeoffs; GPS/GIS vs. mental maps; formal/functional/perceptual regions; expansion/relocation diffusion; distance-decay; time-space compression.

Vocabulary & anchors: choropleth vs. dot density (when each misleads); sustainability frames (intro).

Study focus: pick one world region—compare distortion on Mercator vs. equal-area projection qualitatively.

Common mistakes: GPS point = cultural region; correlation as proof of cause.

Exam tip: name diffusion type + one real example (language, disease, tech) with direction.

topics.title: APHG — Population & migration
topics.content:
Overview: Demographic transition model links development to birth/death rates; migration theories add networks, policies, and stage-specific gender patterns.

Key ideas: push/pull; step migration; refugee vs. asylum distinctions (intro); guest worker programs; brain drain/gain; dependency ratio implications.

Vocabulary & anchors: Malthusian legacy vs. Boserup counteremphasis (high level); anti-natal policies.

Study focus: one policy change → expected effect on labor inflow/outflow with mechanism.

Common mistakes: all migration permanent; ignoring forced migration categories.

Exam tip: chain: economic shock → incentive change → migration stream; add one counterflow if relevant.

topics.title: APHG — Cities & urban land use
topics.content:
Overview: Land rent, transport costs, and zoning produce rings, sectors, or multiple nuclei; rapid urbanization creates informal housing and infrastructure stress.

Key ideas: bid-rent curve; CBD functions; gentrification drivers; squatter settlements; edge cities.

Vocabulary & anchors: primate city; rank-size rule (when it fails); food deserts as accessibility issue.

Study focus: explain why retail bids more near core than single-family housing in classic models.

Common mistakes: forcing every metro into concentric rings.

Exam tip: pair one model prediction with a real city counterexample.

topics.title: APHG — Political geography
topics.content:
Overview: States claim sovereignty over territory; boundaries and supranational organizations complicate control; ethnic geography intersects with electoral and conflict maps.

Key ideas: nation vs. state vs. nation-state; boundaries (antecedent/relict etc. at intro); devolution; supranationalism (EU as example); gerrymandering basics.

Vocabulary & anchors: chokepoints; EEZ concept; shatterbelt regions (name + caution).

Study focus: explain one devolution pressure (economic, ethnic, or logistical) with example region.

Common mistakes: equating ethnicity with automatic separatism.

Exam tip: use maps mentally—describe a spatial pattern before explaining process.

topics.title: AP Psych — Research methods & ethics
topics.content:
Overview: Empirical psychology tests claims with designs that trade internal/external validity; ethics codes add consent, risk/benefit, and debrief norms.

Key ideas: IV/DV, operational definitions, confounds; experimental vs. correlational vs. quasi-experimental; random assignment; statistics vocabulary at descriptive level; IRB role (intro).

Vocabulary & anchors: placebo; double-blind; demand characteristics; replication crisis awareness (nuanced one sentence).

Study focus: given a headline, argue correlation vs. causation with what extra evidence would be needed.

Common mistakes: “prove” from one study; p-hacking mention without understanding—keep to “need replication.”

Exam tip: name one internal validity threat and tie a control to it.

topics.title: AP Psych — Biological bases of behavior
topics.content:
Overview: Neurons communicate chemically and electrically; brain systems specialize but interact; endocrine slow broadcast modulates arousal and metabolism.

Key ideas: action potential phases; synapse; excitatory vs. inhibitory; agonist/antagonist; major lobes; split-brain tasks; plasticity.

Vocabulary & anchors: refractory period; neuroplasticity after stroke; HPA axis stress pathway (sketch).

Study focus: map sleep deprivation to two levels (e.g., attention behavior + autonomic signs).

Common mistakes: one-to-one phrenology-style mapping.

Exam tip: label diagrams left/right hemisphere functions carefully.

topics.title: AP Psych — Learning & memory
topics.content:
Overview: Conditioning shapes voluntary and involuntary associations; memory has stages with encoding strategies and known failure modes.

Key ideas: classical conditioning phases; operant schedules partial reinforcement extinction; observational learning; encoding specificity; interference; reconstructive memory.

Vocabulary & anchors: latent learning; spacing effect; misinformation effect (name).

Study focus: novel vignette—label UCS/UCR/CS/CR cleanly.

Common mistakes: negative reinforcement vs. punishment; proactive vs. retroactive interference flipped.

Exam tip: define term then apply—two sentences minimum per FRQ bullet.

topics.title: AP Psych — Social cognition & personality
topics.content:
Overview: People infer causes for behavior; attitudes predict behavior weakly unless situational pressures dominate; trait and social-cognitive theories organize individual differences.

Key ideas: fundamental attribution error; cognitive dissonance; conformity vs. obedience studies (ethics awareness); Big Five at intro level; locus of control.

Vocabulary & anchors: self-serving bias; stereotype threat (define carefully).

Study focus: identify situational vs. dispositional attribution in a short scenario.

Common mistakes: Milgram as “proving evil”—discuss obedience to authority nuance.

Exam tip: link one classic study to ethical upgrades in modern codes.

topics.title: AP Precalc — Functions & transformations
topics.content:
Overview: Functions model dependence; transformations let you build families from parent graphs; piecewise and composition appear in modeling.

Key ideas: domain/range after shifts/scales; even/odd tests; inverse existence (horizontal line test); composition domain restrictions.

Vocabulary & anchors: asymptotic behavior; end behavior; average rate of change vs. instantaneous preview.

Study focus: write equation for transformed quadratic/exponential from verbal description.

Common mistakes: horizontal shift sign; reflecting about y = x vs. y-axis confusion.

Exam tip: verify inverse with f(f⁻¹(x)) = x on one numeric check.

topics.title: AP Precalc — Trigonometry & models
topics.content:
Overview: The unit circle unifies radians, coordinates, and periodic phenomena; sinusoidal models capture cycles in data.

Key ideas: sine/cosine graphs; amplitude, midline, period, phase shift; inverse trig domains; law of sines/cosines selection rules.

Vocabulary & anchors: angular frequency; simple harmonic motion as sinusoid.

Study focus: fit A, B, C, D in Asin(B(x−C))+D from graph features.

Common mistakes: degree/radian mix; arcsin vs. arccos range.

Exam tip: draw reference triangle before evaluating at non-special angles if allowed.

topics.title: AP Precalc — Sequences & exponential change
topics.content:
Overview: Discrete sequences model steps; exponentials model constant proportional growth or decay over continuous time.

Key ideas: arithmetic vs. geometric explicit/recursive; partial sums; finite geometric series; linking percent change to base (1+r).

Vocabulary & anchors: convergence/divergence language for infinite series (intro only).

Study focus: translate word problem to correct model type before algebra.

Common mistakes: linear fit to exponential data; off-by-one on first term index.

Exam tip: state domain (integer t vs. real t) when interpreting parameters.

topics.title: AP Precalc — Polar & parametric (intro)
topics.content:
Overview: Polar coordinates describe location by angle and radius; parametric equations separate x(t) and y(t) for motion paths.

Key ideas: plotting (r, θ); multiple polar representations; converting polar ↔ rectangular; eliminating parameter for simple curves.

Vocabulary & anchors: limaçon/rose names optional; projectile motion as parametric intro.

Study focus: convert r = 2sin θ to rectangular form for a familiar circle case.

Common mistakes: negative r interpretations; assuming t is always time.

Exam tip: check domain of θ for full trace vs. trace segment.

topics.title: AP Chem — Atomic structure & periodicity
topics.content:
Overview: Quantized light-matter interaction supports electron models; periodic trends summarize effective nuclear charge and shielding patterns—with known exceptions.

Key ideas: photoelectric effect logic; emission/absorption spectra; electron configurations and ions; IE, atomic radius, electronegativity trends; periodicity anomalies (e.g., O vs. F IE).

Vocabulary & anchors: Aufbau exceptions (Cr, Cu as memorization); shielding vs. penetration (qualitative).

Study focus: compare Neighbor elements across a period for radius vs. IE with Zeff reasoning.

Common mistakes: more valence electrons does not always mean larger atom across periods.

Exam tip: one sentence linking trend direction to Zeff and shell added when going down a group.

topics.title: AP Chem — Stoichiometry & solutions
topics.content:
Overview: Mole bridges convert between mass, particles, and solution volumes; limiting reagent logic governs theoretical yield; titrations connect acid–base stoichiometry to concentration unknowns.

Key ideas: roadmap; limiting reactant; percent yield; Molarity; serial dilution; titration equivalence point concept.

Vocabulary & anchors: standard solution; indicator role; back-titration (name only).

Study focus: multi-step limiting reagent with solution volume step.

Common mistakes: molarity vs. molality; volume additivity assumption.

Exam tip: carry units line-by-line; box final answer with correct sig figs rule stated once.

topics.title: AP Chem — IMFs, phases, & properties
topics.content:
Overview: Intermolecular forces determine boiling points, vapor pressure, and solubility “like dissolves like” trends; phase diagrams summarize equilibria.

Key ideas: London dispersion scaling; dipole–dipole; hydrogen bonding conditions; VP–temperature relation; heating curves (plateaus).

Vocabulary & anchors: viscosity; surface tension; critical point (name).

Study focus: rank bp for similar MW molecules by IMF type.

Common mistakes: H-bond drawn through nonpolar H; confusing IMFs with intramolecular bonds.

Exam tip: label all IMFs present on a two-molecule diagram before comparing strength.

topics.title: AP Stats — Exploring one-variable data
topics.content:
Overview: Describing a distribution requires shape, center, spread, and unusual features; choice of summary depends on skew and outliers.

Key ideas: stem/stot/histogram interpretation; mean vs. median resistance; standard deviation vs. IQR roles; boxplot fences for outliers (rule-dependent).

Vocabulary & anchors: modality; uniform vs. skewed; cumulative relative frequency.

Study focus: justify mean vs. median for right-skewed income data.

Common mistakes: outlier without rule; confusing range with IQR.

Exam tip: always mention context when comparing centers (“typical salary” vs. “mean salary”).

topics.title: AP Stats — Two-variable data & models
topics.content:
Overview: Scatterplots reveal direction, form, strength, outliers; correlation measures linear association; regression predicts response with residual analysis.

Key ideas: r properties and limitations; LSRL interpretation of slope/intercept; residual plots for nonlinearity; coefficient of determination language (intro).

Vocabulary & anchors: influential point vs. high leverage (conceptual); extrapolation risk.

Study focus: interpret slope in context with units (y-unit per x-unit).

Common mistakes: correlation implies causation; forcing linear model on curved data.

Exam tip: “For each additional X, predicted Y changes by b” template—customize to context.

topics.title: AP Stats — Designing studies & inference basics
topics.content:
Overview: Random sampling supports population inference; random assignment supports causal claims; bias and variability tradeoffs appear in every design critique.

Key ideas: SRS, stratified, cluster (compare why use); blocking; placebo; double-blind; scope of inference table; p-value and confidence interval logic at conceptual level.

Vocabulary & anchors: confounding; placebo effect; nonresponse bias.

Study focus: identify whether a headline claim needs an experiment vs. observational evidence.

Common mistakes: generalizing beyond sampled population; “accept H₀” language.

Exam tip: first sentence of FRQ names study type and variable roles (explanatory/response).

topics.title: AP Calc AB — Limits & continuity
topics.content:
Overview: Limits describe approaching behavior; continuity ties limits to function values and enables theorems about roots and extremes on intervals.

Key ideas: limit laws; one-sided limits; infinite limits vs. limits at infinity; removable vs. jump vs. infinite discontinuities; IVT conditions.

Vocabulary & anchors: indeterminate forms; sandwich/squeeze at intro level.

Study focus: piecewise function—continuity at junction using left/right limits and f(c).

Common mistakes: plugging before checking domain; mixing limit at ∞ with horizontal asymptote conditions.

Exam tip: show limit notation even if graph suggests the answer.

topics.title: AP Calc AB — Derivatives as rates of change
topics.content:
Overview: Derivative as slope of tangent and as instantaneous rate; basic rules build models for motion, cost, and growth.

Key ideas: limit definition (power examples); product/quotient/chain; implicit differentiation intro; related rates setup checklist.

Vocabulary & anchors: marginal cost/revenue as derivative interpretation; units on derivatives.

Study focus: interpret f′(a) sign and magnitude in applied sentence.

Common mistakes: chain rule inner derivative dropped; related rates missing similar triangle or constraint equation.

Exam tip: write “units of f′ are units of f per unit of x” once per problem.

topics.title: AP Calc AB — Integrals & accumulation (intro)
topics.content:
Overview: Antiderivatives reverse differentiation; definite integrals accumulate signed area and model net change when rate is known.

Key ideas: FTC overview; average value of a function; area between curves setup; u-substitution pattern recognition (intro).

Vocabulary & anchors: initial condition for particular solution; displacement vs. distance from velocity.

Study focus: set up integral for shaded region with limits from intersections.

Common mistakes: negative area below x-axis ignored in total distance problems.

Exam tip: sketch integrand sign before evaluating definite integral for area interpretations.

topics.title: AP Lang — Rhetorical analysis foundations
topics.content:
Overview: Writers choose strategies—appeals, structure, tone, and syntax—to move an audience; your job is to explain how choices create effects, not to agree/disagree casually.

Key ideas: ethos/pathos/logos; occasion, audience, purpose; thesis = commentary on choices, not summary alone; line of reasoning.

Vocabulary & anchors: concession/refutation; anaphora, parallelism (recognize + effect); juxtaposition.

Study focus: write a two-chunk body paragraph: strategy name + quoted evidence + two sentences of effect on audience.

Common mistakes: feature spotting without linking to purpose; vague “appeals to emotion.”

Exam tip: start sentences with strong analytical verbs: “signals,” “frames,” “undercuts.”

topics.title: AP Lang — Argument & evidence
topics.content:
Overview: Defensible claims need limits, evidence types, and acknowledgment of complexity; college-level argument weighs tradeoffs.

Key ideas: qualifying thesis; types of evidence (anecdote, statistic, expert); addressing counterargument; avoiding hasty generalization.

Vocabulary & anchors: straw man vs. steel man (intro); warrant in Toulmin sense.

Study focus: outline a 40-minute argument with thesis + two body areas + rebuttal slot.

Common mistakes: thesis as mere opinion (“X is bad”); evidence dumps without reasoning.

Exam tip: one concrete example per body—not only hypotheticals.

topics.title: AP Lit — Close reading & poetry
topics.content:
Overview: Literary analysis ties diction, imagery, figurative language, and form to themes and tensions; poetry rewards attention to line breaks and meter where relevant.

Key ideas: speaker vs. poet; tone shifts; symbol vs. motif; structure ( volta in sonnet, etc., when applicable).

Vocabulary & anchors: apostrophe, synecdoche (recognition level); ambiguity as interpretive space.

Study focus: write a thesis that names a pattern + a complexity (not one-word theme).

Common mistakes: plot summary; identifying devices without significance.

Exam tip: embed short quotations (≤1 line) and follow the 2:1 ratio—twice as much analysis as quote.

topics.title: AP Lit — Fiction & drama
topics.content:
Overview: Character desire, conflict, setting, and point of view shape meaning; drama adds staging, dialogue, and spectacle constraints.

Key ideas: unreliable narrator signals; setting as pressure on choice; tragic flaw vs. situational irony (use texts carefully).

Vocabulary & anchors: foil; dramatic irony; exposition/rising action labels only when useful.

Study focus: trace one character change across two scenes with two cited moments.

Common mistakes: moralizing instead of analyzing; anachronistic standards.

Exam tip: Q3—pre-outline 5–7 works with themes you can defend in detail.

topics.title: AP CSA — Objects & classes
topics.content:
Overview: Object-oriented design bundles state (fields) and behavior (methods); constructors establish invariants; encapsulation hides internal detail.

Key ideas: class vs. instance; public/private; mutators vs. accessors; this reference; overloaded constructors.

Vocabulary & anchors: static vs. instance; null reference risks; toString/equals contracts (intro).

Study focus: implement a small class (e.g., BankAccount) with deposit/withdraw and no negative balance rule.

Common mistakes: forgetting return type on getters; exposing mutable internal arrays.

Exam tip: trace memory with box-and-pointer diagrams for reference types.

topics.title: AP CSA — Arrays, ArrayList, & algorithms
topics.content:
Overview: Collections store sequences; standard algorithms traverse, search, and mutate with careful bounds and complexity intuition.

Key ideas: 0-based indexing; enhanced for-loop vs. index loop; ArrayList methods; linear search; selection vs. insertion sort idea.

Vocabulary & anchors: off-by-one; defensive copying when returning internal collections.

Study focus: write method to remove all occurrences of value v without skipping after removal.

Common mistakes: Concurrent modification patterns; comparing arrays with ==.

Exam tip: write pre/post conditions in comments before coding FRQ-style methods.

topics.title: AP Phys 1 — Kinematics & dynamics
topics.content:
Overview: Motion diagrams and graphs link position, velocity, acceleration; Newton’s laws connect forces to motion vectors for particles and simple systems.

Key ideas: SUVAT subset for constant a; projectile independence; free-body diagrams; friction models; Atwood/string tension patterns at intro.

Vocabulary & anchors: inertia; net force; apparent weight in accelerating elevator.

Study focus: multi-object system—identify third-law pairs vs. system external forces.

Common mistakes: mixing up velocity and acceleration directions; double-counting forces on a string.

Exam tip: draw FBD before choosing axis tilt for inclined planes.

topics.title: AP Phys 1 — Energy & momentum
topics.content:
Overview: Work–energy tracks scalar accumulation; momentum is conserved in isolated collisions; rotation adds torque and angular momentum later in course.

Key ideas: work by constant force; kinetic energy; Hooke’s law spring PE; mechanical energy with nonconservative work; impulse-momentum theorem; elastic vs. inelastic collisions.

Vocabulary & anchors: negative work meaning; center of mass velocity for isolated system.

Study focus: energy bar charts for spring-mass oscillation qualitative cycles.

Common mistakes: using conservation of energy when friction does net work on system—check.

Exam tip: define system boundary before claiming momentum conservation.

topics.title: APES — Earth systems & ecology
topics.content:
Overview: Matter cycles and energy flow connect sun to producers to consumers; populations interact through competition, predation, and symbiosis within changing abiotic constraints.

Key ideas: primary productivity vs. biomass; trophic efficiency ~10% rule; carrying capacity; logistic growth; disturbance and succession (intro).

Vocabulary & anchors: keystone species; niche partitioning; albedo feedback (name).

Study focus: explain one human land-use change through a food-web consequence.

Common mistakes: sustainability as slogan only—tie to throughput limits.

Exam tip: label axes on population graphs (time vs. N or dN/dt vs. N for logistic).

topics.title: APES — Human impacts & policy
topics.content:
Overview: Pollution, resource extraction, and climate-forcing emissions create tradeoffs; policy tools mix standards, taxes, cap-and-trade, and restoration.

Key ideas: point vs. nonpoint source; PM2.5 health; greenhouse effect mechanism; mitigation vs. adaptation; tragedy of the commons applied to fisheries.

Vocabulary & anchors: externalized costs; environmental justice framing (define carefully).

Study focus: compare command-and-control vs. market incentive for one pollutant.

Common mistakes: conflating ozone hole with climate change mechanism.

Exam tip: use one quantitative magnitude as illustration (cite general ranges, not fake precision).
